Most of us are not CEOs of Fortune 500 companies so this 1:1 advice does not apply to most. I hope most managers don't try to emulate it. 1:1s are not for status updates or dumping problems on your manager. They are for building a connection between manager-report, for aligning on shared vision, for learning from each other and for coaching each other. Any manager-report relationship needs all this. 1:1s don't have to be an hour weekly. They can be 30 mins biweekly if time is precious. By having them regularly and showing up on time and being attentive you are sending a lot of signals to your report that you care.

The way tech companies organize their teams is changing fast. The old model of “general managers” running business units is on its way out. In its place, more companies are moving back to a functional management model — grouping teams by domain like design, engineering, or sales. Block made headlines this year when Jack Dorsey told employees: “We’re going to reorganize our entire company by function and disband our business unit reporting structure.” Airbnb has followed the same path. Brian Chesky explained: “At Airbnb … we have a head of design, a head of engineering, a head of product marketing … we don’t have these little pods.” This shift raises the bar for managers. They can no longer be generalists who know a little about everything. They now need functional expertise in the domain they manage. An engineering manager must know engineering. A design manager must know design. Without that depth, they can’t coach their teams, spot real quality, or make the right calls under pressure. The benefits are significant: • Faster decisions because leaders understand the work firsthand • Higher standards because experts know what high performance looks like • CEOs gain a direct line into each function and stay closer to the details I’m seeing the same trend with our customers. When managers are true domain experts, teams move quicker, quality goes up, and organizations stay more agile. General managers are fading. Domain experts are rising. For fast-growing tech companies, this is becoming the new winning strategy.

The single best decision an outgoing CEO can make is to find a better replacement. In 2014, Microsoft was crumbling. Windows was fading, the Nokia acquisition had cost them $7.2 billion, and competitors like Apple and Google were dominating. The stock was stagnant. Then came Satya Nadella. A soft-spoken engineer from inside the company, his ascent marked one of the greatest corporate turnarounds in history. He didn't just save Microsoft; he transformed it into a $3 trillion empire by pivoting away from the past and betting everything on the future. His strategy was a masterclass in reinvention: → He shifted to a "cloud-first, mobile-first" model, investing heavily in Azure when others were skeptical. → He transformed Office from a one-time purchase into the subscription-based Office 365, creating a recurring revenue machine. → He partnered with former enemies like Apple and Linux, strengthening Microsoft’s ecosystem instead of fighting losing battles. → He acquired strategically, buying not just companies but entire ecosystems: LinkedIn ($26B), GitHub ($7.5B), and Activision Blizzard ($68B). Today, Microsoft leads in cloud computing, enterprise software, and AI. This wasn't luck; it was the intentional vision of one leader. Here are 7 powerful lessons from Satya Nadella’s playbook: 1️⃣ Adapt or Die: Your current success can make you complacent. Disrupt yourself before a competitor does it for you. 2️⃣ Bet on the Future: Invest in next-generation trends (like Cloud and AI), not just what works today. 3️⃣ Culture > Strategy: Nadella replaced Microsoft’s combative internal culture with a "learn-it-all" growth mindset. Yet velocity kept dropping. Projects stalled. Deadlines slipped. The energy that once drove late-night breakthroughs had simply... vanished. Digging deeper revealed 3 silent killers: 1. Silence means "yes" (but actually means "I'm terrified"). Three people knew about that ₹40 lakh mistake that it wouldn't work. None spoke up. The most expensive mistake we see in high-growth companies isn't overhiring or underinvesting. It's building systems that work perfectly at their current size—and catastrophically at 2X scale. We call it the "Scale Cliff." Some warning signs you're approaching one: ✓ Communication depends on tribal knowledge ✓ Decision workflows rely on 2-3 key people ✓ Your tech stack shows early signs of strain ✓ Quick fixes outnumber strategic solutions ✓ Teams are solving symptoms, not root causes We observed this firsthand when a rapidly growing fintech increased from 50 to 200 employees in 14 months. What broke first? Not their product or customer acquisition. Their knowledge management and decision systems collapsed. Meetings multiplied. Context was lost. New hires took months to become productive. Priorities changed weekly. Their systems weren't designed to distribute decision-making at scale. The irony? The team was brilliant. The product was growing. The market was strong. But their operating system couldn't handle the complexity. Smart leaders recognize that bottlenecks are actually systems problems in disguise. The question isn't "How do we grow faster?" It's "What systems would need to exist for growth to feel effortless?" Systems thinking isn't just for engineers. It's the hidden advantage of every team that's successfully scaled. A.G.A.I.N #23 – A Good Advice I Neglected: “You can’t lead on autopilot.” One of the most common patterns I see with founders is this: they’re everywhere and nowhere at the same time. Juggling investor calls, firefighting operations, hiring, expansion, product. They’re stretched thin. The calendar is full, but the team is still waiting — for clarity, for direction, for a decision. When people don’t get time with their leaders, the gap fills up with confusion. They start guessing priorities, second-guessing each other, and third-guessing themselves. The problem isn’t intent. It’s bandwidth. When founders (or anyone in leadership) are too busy to sit down with their teams, everything slows down — except anxiety. What works isn’t more effort. It’s structured attention. Portion clear time for what actually builds clarity — reviews, check-ins, and one-time projects that set direction or fix systemic issues. Be hands-on where the fundamentals are being built. Don’t delegate the decisions that define direction. There are ways to make limited founder time go further. → Detailed notes and clear summaries: Every project and process update email should call out decisions needed, blockers, and key takeaways. Don’t make people read through threads to find the point. → Escalations that are documented: Use WhatsApp for urgency, but always follow up with an email for continuity and retention. What gets buried in a chat disappears from collective memory. → Broad meetings with the right people: Include cross-functional stakeholders so context travels horizontally, not just top-down. A good meeting saves twenty clarifying ones later. → Be concise: If you’re working with a founder, lead with a TL;DR that captures the issue, the decision required, and key context. Make it easy for them to say yes or no quickly, and then dive into details. You’ll save time, get faster responses, and reduce back-and-forth. People don’t need constant access. They need consistent clarity. You don’t have to be everywhere. You just have to show up when it counts. Because when leaders run on autopilot, everyone else is left circling the runway. PC: IKEA on a toasty October morning in Bangalore. Yes, we are in that part of the year where living in Bangalore makes sense.

WeWork filed for bankruptcy in 2024. $47 billion in value. Gone. The headlines blame market conditions and bad timing. But I see something else: the cost of leading without emotional intelligence. I’ve watched this pattern destroy companies from the inside out. Adam Neumann, WeWork’s founder, reportedly fired employees in elevators. He told staff they weren’t “hustling hard enough” while burning through $100 million a month. When investors questioned him, he doubled down instead of listening. Meanwhile, Satya Nadella was quietly transforming Microsoft. Same market pressures. Same competitive landscape. Completely different approach. When Nadella took over in 2014, Microsoft was stuck. The company culture was toxic. Teams hoarded information instead of sharing it. His first move wasn’t a strategy deck or a reorganization. He started with three words: “I was wrong.” Then he did something that changed everything. He made vulnerability a leadership strength. The way tech companies organize their teams is changing fast. The old model of “general managers” running business units is on its way out. In its place, more companies are moving back to a functional management model — grouping teams by domain like design, engineering, or sales. Block made headlines this year when Jack Dorsey told employees: “We’re going to reorganize our entire company by function and disband our business unit reporting structure.” Airbnb has followed the same path. Brian Chesky explained: “At Airbnb … we have a head of design, a head of engineering, a head of product marketing … we don’t have these little pods.” This shift raises the bar for managers. They can no longer be generalists who know a little about everything. They now need functional expertise in the domain they manage. An engineering manager must know engineering. A design manager must know design. Without that depth, they can’t coach their teams, spot real quality, or make the right calls under pressure. The benefits are significant: • Faster decisions because leaders understand the work firsthand • Higher standards because experts know what high performance looks like • CEOs gain a direct line into each function and stay closer to the details I’m seeing the same trend with our customers. When managers are true domain experts, teams move quicker, quality goes up, and organizations stay more agile. General managers are fading. Domain experts are rising. For fast-growing tech companies, this is becoming the new winning strategy.
